Alright, let's talk about the first A in the three As of building a new behavior. So, achievable. How do we make habits and behaviors more achievable? How do we make them more possible in something that we can actually stick to? So there's a really great book by a woman named Twyla Tharp and the book is called The Creative Habit. I'm going to read you a section from it here. Now Tharp is one of the best choreographers and dance instructors that we have in the world right now. She's one of the art genius of Grant. She's done a variety of shows across Broadway, many other really spectacular plays. So she's highly respecting her field. And she does a really interesting thing with her morning routine and the habits that she sticks to. So, let me read the quote to you now. She says, I begin each day of my life with a ritual. I wake up at 5.30 a.m., put on my workout clothes, my leg warmers, my sweatshirt, and my hat. I walk outside my Manhattan home, hail a taxi, and tell the driver to take me to the pumping iron gym at 91st Street and 1st Avenue, where I work out for two hours. The ritual is not the stretching and weight training that I put my body through each morning at the gym. The ritual is the cab. The moment I tell the driver where to go, I've completed the ritual. It's a simple act, but doing it the same way each morning habitualize it, makes it repeatable and easy to do. It reduces the chance that I would skip it or do it differently. It's one more item in my arsenal of routines and one less thing to think about. All right, so what's interesting about Tharp's action here is that she's focusing on making the very beginning of the habit as achievable as possible. The only thing that she puts her energy into is hailing the cab each morning, and then she trusts that the rest of the action, the rest of the behavior, will follow from that. As long as she hails the cab and gets in it, she knows she's going to show up at the gym and start working out and continue the rest of the routine, because it's already started this kind of cascade of behavior. This is one of the most important things that I think we can cover when it comes to making habits more achievable, which is that so often we think about the entire habit that we want to build, this big thing, this goal that we want to achieve. If we think about the habits in their full scope, then they can be intimidating. It can seem like too much. Well, instead of thinking about focusing on working out for two hours, Tharp shifts her focus to just hailing the cab, something that she could do in two minutes. This is what I like to call the two-minute rule. The two-minute rule is the idea that not all habits can be accomplished in two minutes, but pretty much any habit can be started in two minutes. The goal of the two-minute rule is to figure out a way for you to start your behavior to initiate the habit in two minutes or less. If it takes less than two minutes, then it adheres to the rule. An example, you get home from work, maybe you want to go for a three-mile run. That sounds really good, but once you get home from work, you're exhausted, you're tired, and the idea of going for a 45-minute run or a 30-minute run or however long it would be seems intimidating to you. Thinking about the habit in its full scope seems like too much work. Instead, focus only on the first two minutes, putting on your shoes, tying them, getting your water bottle, and stepping out the door and closing the door. If you do that, if you do something that you can actually accomplish within the first two minutes, then it doesn't matter if you do the rest. Literally, you can make a promise to yourself, once you step out the door and close it, you can go right back in and you're done. It doesn't matter at that point. What often happens is that once the behavior is initiated, once you start, then you end up finishing the behavior anyway. The key distinction here is that motivation often comes after starting a behavior, not before. We think that motivation is something we need to have, oh, if I don't feel motivated enough to work out for 45 minutes, then I can't follow through on that habit. In fact, if we can focus only on the first two minutes, then we can trust that the rest of the behavior will follow, just like Twylethar pailing the cap. What I'd like you to do now is to think about how to make your behavior easier at first, even if it's not as impressive. Click on the worksheet below this video, write down the habits that you want to build, and then write down a two-minute version of how you can just kickstart that habit and only focus on the first two minutes, rather than thinking about the entire scope of the behavior. Go ahead and do that now. I'll see you in the next lesson.
